coredata.dc:title: Magnetic molecularly imprinted microspheres and carbon dots based semi-homogeneous fluorescence method for detection of roxarsone and arsanilic acid in animal feeds
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jia Peng, Yao
Feng, Cheng
Lin, Min
Lin Liu, Ge
Su, Yan
Zhe Hou, Hao
Ping Wang, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Roxarsone and arsanilic acid are all organoarsenic compounds, and they are usually used as feed additives. However, their residues in foods of animal origin and their metabolites distributing into the environment are the public concerns. The inspection of their appearances in animal feeds can avoid the above problems. In this study, a type of magnetic molecularly imprinted microsphere was synthesized that was combined with a carbon-dots labeled fluorescent tracer to develop a semi-homogeneous fluorescence method for determination of the two drugs in animal feeds. Results showed this method contained only one step (sample-loading), the limits of detection for roxarsone and arsanilic acid were 0.02 and 0.12 mg/kg respectively, and the magnetic microsphere could be reused for 4 times. In addition, the method sensitivity when using carbon-dots labeled fluorescent tracer was higher than that when using fluorescein isothiocyanate labeled fluorescent tracer. This method exhibited better performances than the previous immunoassays and molecularly imprinted polymer-based method for determination of the two analytes. So this method could be used as a simple tool for screening of roxarsone and arsanilic acid in animal feeds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Roxarsone
Arsanilic acid
Magnetic molecularly imprinted microsphere
Carbon-dots labeled fluorescent tracer
Semi-homogeneous fluorescence method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of magnetic molecularly imprinted microsphere (MMIM)
Evaluation of MMIM and fluorescent tracers

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecules of the tested drugs, and the operation route of the MMIM based semi-homogeneous fluorescence method.
Characterization of MMIM by using (A) SEM, (B) TEM and (C) VSM. (D) Adsorption amounts of MMIM for the tested drugs (magnetic material 5 mg, drug 10 μg/mL 5 mL).
Results for evaluation of MMIM (10 μg/well) and two fluorescent tracers (10 μg/mL) (AM=amoxicillin, CL=clenbuterol, SA=sulfadiazine, FL=florfenicol).
Competitive curves of (A) ROX fortified blank chicken feed at different dilutions when using ASA-CD and (B) matrix matched ROX when using the two fluorescent tracers (0.0001–50 μg/mL).
Comparisons with the rapid screening immunoassays for ROX and ASA.

head.ce:title: Magnetic molecularly imprinted microspheres and carbon dots based semi-homogeneous fluorescence method for detection of roxarsone and arsanilic acid in animal feeds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Veterinary Medicine, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding Hebei 071000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Veterinary Medicine, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding Hebei 071000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding Hebei 071000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding Hebei 071000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Roxarsone and arsanilic acid are all organoarsenic compounds, and they are usually used as feed additives. However, their residues in foods of animal origin and their metabolites distributing into the environment are the public concerns. The inspection of their appearances in animal feeds can avoid the above problems. In this study, a type of magnetic molecularly imprinted microsphere was synthesized that was combined with a carbon-dots labeled fluorescent tracer to develop a semi-homogeneous fluorescence method for determination of the two drugs in animal feeds. Results showed this method contained only one step (sample-loading), the limits of detection for roxarsone and arsanilic acid were 0.02 and 0.12 mg/kg respectively, and the magnetic microsphere could be reused for 4 times. In addition, the method sensitivity when using carbon-dots labeled fluorescent tracer was higher than that when using fluorescein isothiocyanate labeled fluorescent tracer. This method exhibited better performances than the previous immunoassays and molecularly imprinted polymer-based method for determination of the two analytes. So this method could be used as a simple tool for screening of roxarsone and arsanilic acid in animal feeds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Roxarsone (ROX) and arsanilic acid (ASA) (Fig. 1 ) are organoarsenic drugs that have been used as feed additives for food producing animals to promote animal growth, improve feed efficiency and control intestinal parasites for more than 70 years [1,2]. ROX and ASA are low toxic compounds, but they can be metabolized to the more toxic inorganic metabolites by gut microflora [3,4]. More than 90 % of organoarsenic drugs are excreted as parent formations [5], but they can be degraded to the more toxic metabolites soon after entering the environment [6,7]. So the residues of ROX and ASA in foods of animal origin are the large risks to the consumers [8], and their metabolites in soil are toxic to the environmental organisms [9]. In addition, the metabolites of ROX and ASA deposited in soil can be absorbed by vegetables [10], which are also the risks to the consumers.

For the protection of consumer health, ROX has been banned as feed additive in the European Union and the United States of America, and ROX and ASA have been banned as feed additives in China. Therefore, the inspection of their presences in animal feeds and foods of animal origin are very necessary. During the past several years, liquid chromatography [11,12], gas chromatography [13], liquid chromatography mass spectrometry [14], electrochemical sensor [15–17], enzyme linked immunosorbent assay [18,19], immunosensor [20], and fluorescence immunoassay [21] have been reported to detect ROX and/or ASA in animal feeds and meat samples.

Among these methods, the chromatography related methods require expensive instruments and professional operators [11–14], and the various sensors require different complicated preparation processes [15–17]. In contrast, immunoassays are simple, cheap and rapid, so they can be used for rapid screening of large number of samples [18–21]. All of the above-mentioned immunoassays use conventional antibodies as the recognition elements, but the antibody generation process requires many laboratory animals and the production procedures are labor-intensive and expensive.

Molecularly imprinted polymer is the synthetic material that also contains specific recognition ability. Compared with the production of an antibody, the synthesis of a molecularly imprinted polymer needs only several days, the procedures are simple and cheap, and the synthesized polymer is recyclable. Therefore, molecularly imprinted polymer has been used as “plastic antibody” to detect many analytes [22]. In our recent reports, molecularly imprinted polymers have been used to develop some pseudo immunoassays for determination of pyrethroids [23], benzimidazoles [24], Sudan dyes [25], chloramphenicol [26], and sulfonamides [27]. To our best knowledge, there have been two articles reporting the synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymer for extraction of ROX [28,29], and there has been only one article reporting the use of molecularly imprinted polymer as recognition element for determination of ROX [30].

For the microplate-based immunoassay, the coating microplate, blocking microplate and several cycles of washing steps are required, and the antibody-coated microplate is not recyclable. In our recent reports, two types of magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers are synthesized that are used to develop two semi-homogeneous colorimetric methods on 96-wll microplate for determination of pentachlorophenol [31] and progestins [32], and the above-mentioned problems are resolved. For the two methods, the magnetic recognition element, the enzyme labeled conjugate and the analyte solution are directly added into microplate wells for competition. The magnetic complexes are separated by using a bottom magnet, then the substrate is added, and the signal is recorded. Finally, the magnetic recognition elements are regenerated for the next use. Therefore, such semi-homogeneous method is simple, the assay time is short, the recognition element can be reused, and it has the potential to achieve automatic operation.

However, the above two semi-homogeneous methods still require a substate to show the signal. In contrast, fluorescence method does not require a substrate because the fluorophore labeled tracer is the signal source. For the conventional fluorescence immunoassay, fluoresceins are the commonly used fluorophores [33]. In the recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have been used as a class of novel fluorophores to develop many fluorescence methods [34]. To our best knowledge, however, there has been no article reporting the use of magnetic molecularly imprinted polymer and CDs-based fluorescent tracer to develop a semi-homogeneous fluorescence method so far. In the present study, ROX was used as template to synthesize a type of magnetic molecularly imprinted microsphere (MMIM), and ASA was coupled with a type of CDs to prepare a fluorescent tracer (ASA-CD). Then a semi-homogeneous fluorescence method was developed on 96-well microplate. As shown in Fig. 1, the MMIM, the ASA-CD and the analyte were directly mixed in microplate wells for competition. After that, the magnetic complexes were adsorbed by using a 96-well bottom magnet, the solutions in the wells were discarded, and the fluorescence signal was excited. Finally, the analytes adsorbed on the MMIM were eluted, and the MMIM was used for the next assay. After optimization of several assay parameters, this method was used to detect ROX and ASA in chicken feed and porcine feed samples.

The standards of ROX and ASA were supplied by Macklin Inc. (Shanghai, China). Divinylbenzene (DVB) and Fe3O4 were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 2,2-azobis (isobutyronitrile) (AIBN) and methacrylic acid (MA) were supplied by Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). The carboxylfunctionalized carbon dots (CD450) were supplied by Xi’an Qiyue Biology Co., Ltd (Xi’an, China). Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) was supplied by Shanghai Yuanye Biological Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of magnetic molecularly imprinted microsphere (MMIM)
ce:section.ce:para: The magnetic molecularly imprinted microsphere (MMIM) was synthesized as the following procedures. Briefly, the template (ROX, 1.0 mmol), the monomer (MA, 4.0 mmol) and 10 mL acetonitrile were added into a three-necked flask to be pre-polymerized for 4 h at room temperature. Then the crosslinker (DVB, 20 mmol), the initiator (AIBN, 50 mg) and 40 mg Fe3O4 were added into the above solution. The mixture was blown with nitrogen for 30 min, and the flask was capped and put onto a heating mantle to be stirred at 60 °C for 12 h. After that, the supernatant was discarded under the help of a magnet, and the obtained magnetic particles were extracted in a Soxhlet apparatus for 3 days by using methanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v). Finally, the MMIM particles were rinsed with water and dried for the subsequent use. As a control, a type of magnetic non-imprinted microsphere (MNIM) was also synthesized as the above procedures but without adding ROX.

The MMIM was characterized by using scanning electron microscope, transmission electron microscope, and vibrating sample magnetometry respectively. Its adsorption specificities for ROX, ASA and several other drugs (amoxicillin, clenbuterol, sulfadiazine, and florfenicol) were determined because they showed some structural similarity (Fig. 1). During the experiments, MMIM (5 mg) was put into the analyte solution (10 μg/mL, 5 mL, single analyte or analyte mixture), and the suspension was incubated for 1 h at room temperature. The supernatant was pipetted out with the help of a magnet, and the analyte concentration in the supernatant was determined by using UV spectrophotometer. Then the adsorption capacity for each drug was calculated.

The amino group in ASA molecule was linked with the carboxylgroup on CD450 to synthesize a fluorescent tracer. Briefly, 1.0 mg CD450, 1.0 mg EDC and 1.0 mg NHS were added into 2 mL methanol to be incubated for 2 h. Then 1 mL methanol containing 5.0 mg ASA was added into the above solution to be stirred gently at 4 °C for 6 h. The mixture was ultrafiltered by using 100 kDa membrane filter to remove the unreacted molecules to obtain the fluorescent tracer ASA-CD. As a control, ASA was also coupled with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) to synthesize the second fluorescent tracer. Briefly, 5 mg ASA and 10 mg FITC were added into 1.0 mL methanol to be stirred gently for 12 h at room temperature. The obtained mixture was transferred onto a thin-layer chromatography plate for separation. Then the silica gel band containing ASA-FITC (Rf value 0.21) was collected and eluted with methanol to obtain the controlled fluorescent tracer ASA-FITC.

The operation route of the semi-homogeneous fluorescence method was shown in Fig. 1. Five milligram MMIM was put into 5 mL PBS (0.01 mol/L, pH 7.5), the suspension was sonicated for 5 min, and 50 μL of the suspension (containing 5 μg MMIM) was pipetted into the microplate well. Then 50 μL fluorescent tracer and 50 μL analyte solution (or sample extract) were added into the wells for incubation for 10 min at 37 °C. The magnetic complexes were separated from the suspension with the help of a 96-well bottom magnet, and the supernatants in the wells were discarded. The left magnetic complexes were rinsed with water for 3 cycles. Then the microplate was read with a multimode reader to record the fluorescence intensity value (FI) of each well (λex 360/λem 450 nm when using ASA-CD, λex 485/λem 528 nm when using ASA-FITC). Finally, all of the magnetic materials in the wells were collected and eluted with 20 mL methanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v) for 5 cycles to remove the adsorbed analytes and tracers for the next use. During the experiments, the determination parameters (IC50, the analyte concentration showing 50 % of inhibition; limit of detection (LOD), the analyte concentration showing 10 % of inhibition) for the two analytes when using ASA-FITC and ASA-CD were determined separately.

The chicken feed and porcine feed samples were extracted as follows. Briefly, 5.0 g minced feed sample and 20 mL methanol were added into a centrifuge tube to be stirred on a variable speed reciprocal shaker for 30 min. The tube was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min, and an appropriate volume of the supernatant was pipetted out and diluted for 10 folds with PBS to be analyzed directly. For this sample extraction method, the analyte concentration in the feed sample was diluted for 40 folds.

Some chicken feed and porcine feed samples were supplied by the controlled feed manufacturers known to be without adding ROX and ASA, and these samples were used as blank samples to evaluate the present method. During the experiments, ROX and ASA were spiked into the blank feed samples (1, 10, 100 mg/kg) to be extracted and analyzed as described above. The intra-assay recoveries of each analyte at each fortification level were the mean recoveries of six repetitions in one day, and the inter-assay recoveries were the mean recoveries of duplicate tests on six successive days. Finally, 50 chicken feed samples and 50 porcine feed samples collected from different farms were analyzed as described above.

The HPLC confirmation was according to a previous report [35]. The analysis was performed on Waters 1525 liquid chromatography system, which was equipped with a Waters 2998 DAD detector and a C18 column (150 × 4.6 mm, 5 μm). The mobile phase was the mixture of 1 % acetic acid/methanol (97:3, v/v) at isocratic elution mode. Other parameters were: flow-rate 0.7 mL/min, injection volume 20 μ L, detector wavelength 260 nm.

This study for the first time synthesized a type of magnetic molecularly imprinted polymer for ROX and ASA. After synthesis, it was characterized by using several techniques. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the characterization result of scanning electron microscope revealed the obtained products were spheric beads, and their size (mean diameter 230 nm) was larger than Fe3O4 (mean diameter 140 nm), indicating the polymer layer was coated on Fe3O4 surface. As shown in Fig. 2B, the characterization result of transmission electron microscope revealed the obtained product was core–shell structured material, indicating the MMIM was obtained. As shown in Fig. 2C, the characterization result of vibrating sample magnetometry revealed this material has magnetism because its hysteresis loop was similar to that of Fe3O4. The three points indicated the MMIM was obtained, so it was used for the subsequent experiments.

There were three articles reporting the synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymer for ROX, but they exhibited no recognition ability for ASA [28–30]. In the present study, the adsorption amounts of the MMIM for ROX, ASA and four other drugs were determined. As shown in Fig. 2D, the adsorption amounts for ROX (45.2 μg) and ASA (43.6 μg) were much higher than that for other drugs (≤5.2 μg). These results revealed that the MMIM was specific for ROX and ASA, and its recognition spectrum was broader than the previous three molecularly imprinted polymers. In contrast, the adsorption amounts of the controlled MNIM for all of the tested six drugs were comparably low (4.5–6.4 μg), indicating it was a non-specific material. In addition, the adsorption amounts of the MMIM for the mixtures of ROX/ASA and other drugs (46.8–48.3 μg, Fig. 2D) were a little higher than that for single ROX and single ASA, which revealed the MMIM could endure the co-existed impurities. In contrast, the adsorption amounts of the controlled MNIM for these mixtures were general the sum of the adsorption amounts for the single analyte, which were from the non-specific adsorptions. So the MMIM was used as the specific recognition element to develop the method for determination of ROX and ASA.

The structure of ASA showed high similarity to ROX (Fig. 1), so ASA was used to synthesize the two fluorescent tracers. In the previous CDs-based fluorescence immunoassays, the CDs were usually coupled with different antibodies to prepare the fluorescent tracers [36–39]. In the present study, the CD450 was directly coupled with ASA to synthesize the fluorescent tracer. As shown in Figure S1A, the UV full wavelength scanning revealed that the curve of ASA-CD contained the characters of ASA and CD450, indicating ASA was linked with CD450 and the ASA-CD was obtained. As shown in Figure S1B, the fluorescence spectrum of ASA-CD (λex 352/λem 440 nm) were similar to that of CD450 (λex 362/λem 450 nm), indicating ASA-CD was a fluorescent material. As shown in Figure S1C, the result of Fourier transform infrared revealed that the ASA-CD contained the peaks of CD450 (N-H bond in –NH2, 3419 cm−1; O-H bond in –COOH, 2906 cm−1; C=C bond in benzene ring, 1550 cm−1), ASA (O-H bond in –COOH, 2959 cm−1; C=C bond in benzene ring, 1554 cm−1; As-O bond, 845 cm−1) and the newly formed N-H bond in –CONH (3337 cm−1). The above results revealed the fluorescent tracer ASA-CD was synthesized. For the ASA-FITC, the UV scanning curves proved ASA was linked with FITC (Figure S1D), the fluorescence spectra proved it was a fluorescent material (Figure S1E), and the mass spectrometry characterization results proved its theoretic molecule weight (606.5) was identical with the tested molecule weight (MS (ESI) m/z: 609.8 ([M+3H]+), Figure S1F). These results revealed the controlled fluorescent tracer ASA-FITC was also obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of MMIM and fluorescent tracers
ce:section.ce:para: For evaluation of the above reagents, the MMIM (10 μg/well) and the ASA-CD (10 μg/mL) were mixed with ROX, ASA and the four other drugs to perform semi-homogeneous competition respectively. When the concentrations of the six analytes were zero, the FI values were comparably high (Fig. 3 ), which meant the MMIM could bind the ASA-CD. When the concentration was 1.0 μg/mL, ASA and ROX showed high inhibition ratios (1-FI/FI0, 56 % and 42 %) whereas the four other drugs showed negligible inhibition ratios (<2%). These results proved the MMIM and the ASA-CD could be used to develop a competitive detection method for ASA and ROX.

During the experiments, ASA-FITC (10 μg/mL) was also used to perform the above experiments. As shown in Fig. 3, the ASA-FITC could also bind with the MMIM, and the FI values (analyte concentration zero) were higher than that when using ASA-CD, which was because the fluorescence quantum yield of FITC (>90 %) was higher than the used CDs (about 35 %). However, the inhibition ratios for ROX (47 %) and ASA (35 %) were lower than that when using the ASA-CD, and the inhibition ratios for other drugs were similar to that when using the ASA-CD. These results proved that the ASA-FITC could also combine with the MMIM to develop a specific detection method for the two drugs, but the sensitivity was lower than that when using the ASA-CD. ASA-CD was a type of nanoparticle and ASA-FITC was a type of soluble compound, so the Brownian movement of ASA-CD was slower than ASA-FITC. In this method, the tracer (ASA-CD or ASA-FITC) and the analyte competed to bind with the MMIM, and in such a case ASA-CD showed weaker binding ability for the MMIM than ASA-FITC. As the results, the binding ability of the analyte for the MMIM when using ASA-CD was higher than that when using ASA-FITC, thus obtaining high inhibition ratio and high sensitivity. So, the MMIM and the ASA-CD were used for the subsequent experiments.

During the experiments, several assay parameters were optimized. First, the concentrations of MMIM and ASA-CD were optimized. During the experiments, different amounts of MMIM (2–20 μg/well) were added into microplate wells, and ROX (1.0 μg/mL) was mixed with different concentrations ASA-CD to be assayed (2–20 μg/mL, incubation 30 min, PBS pH 7.0). Results showed the use of 5.0 μg/well of MMIM and 5.0 μg/mL of ASA-CD achieved the highest inhibition ratio (Figure S2A), so the two concentrations were determined as the optimal conditions. Second, the assay solution PBS was adjusted to different pH to perform the assay (MMIM 5.0 μg/well, ASA-CD 5.0 μg/mL, ROX 1.0 μg/mL, incubation 30 min). Results showed the optimal solution pH was 7.5 (Figure S2B). Third, the influence of incubation time was evaluated (MMIM 5.0 μg/well, ASA-CD 5.0 μg/mL, ROX 1.0 μg/mL, PBS pH 7.5). Results showed the optimal competition time was 10 min (Figure S2C). Under the above optimal conditions, four portions of MMIM were regenerated for successive 7 times to evaluate its cycling performance. Results showed the inhibition ratios of ROX changed a litter when the MMIMs were reused for 4 times, and rapidly decreased thereafter (Figure S2D), so the MMIM was reused for 4 times in the following experiments.

For evaluation of the sample extraction method, ROX was prepared with the extracts of blank chicken feed and diluted for 5–40 folds to be analyzed respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the competitive inhibition curve of matrix matched ROX after 40-folds dilution was identical with that of ROX standard, indicating the influence of sample impurities could be omitted after 40-folds dilution. In such a case, the IC50 values (ROX 0.05 μg/mL and ASA 0.2 μg/mL) and the LODs (ROX 0.0005 μg/mL and ASA 0.003 μg/mL) were calculated. Counting in the 40-folds dilution, the LODs for determination of ROX and ASA in feed sample were 0.02 and 0.12 mg/kg respectively.

During the experiments, the IC50 values (ROX 0.19 μg/mL and ASA 0.8 μg/mL) and the LODs (ROX 0.001 μg/mL and ASA 0.01 μg/mL) when using ASA-FITC were also calculated, which meant the LODs for detection of ROX and ASA in feed sample were 0.04 and 0.4 mg/kg respectively. These results revealed that both of the two fluorescent tracers could combine with the MMIM to detect low level of ROX and ASA in animal feed, but the use of ASA-CD achieved a higher sensitivity. The competitive curves of matrix matched ROX when using the two fluorescent tracers also proved this point (Fig. 4B). Then ROX and ASA were fortified into the blank chicken feed and porcine feed samples to be extracted and assayed, and the recoveries (62.5 %-85.1 %) and the coefficients of variation (6.2 %-13.5 %) were shown in Table S1.

The 100 unknown feed samples were assayed by the present method, and only one chicken feed sample was determined as positive (23.2 mg/kg, expressed as ROX). However, the specific analyte was unknown because the MMIM could not distinguish ROX from ASA. So these unknown samples were further analyzed by the HPLC method. The HPLC results showed the above positive chicken feed sample contained ROX (25.1 mg/kg), and other unknown samples were negative samples. This revealed the present semi-homogeneous fluorescent method was accurate that could be used as a screening tool for rapid determination of ROX and ASA in animal feeds.

There were several immunoassays [18–21] and one molecularly imprinted polymer-based method [30] reported to detect ROX or ASA in animal feeds and animal derived foods (Table 1 ). First, the synthesis of MMIM was simpler and cheaper than the productions of the monoclonal antibody and polyclonal antibody used in those immunoassays. Second, the ELISA [18,19] and the fluorescence immunoassay [21] required long-time of microplate coating step, whereas the present method did not require the step. Third, the present method was recyclable, but the ELISA and the fluorescence immunoassay were not reusable. Fourth, the assay time of the present method was shorter than all of those immunoassays. Fifth, the present method could detect ROX and ASA simultaneously, but all of those immunoassays could only detect one analyte. Sixth, the sensitivity of the present method was much higher than that of the ELISA for determination of ASA in feeds [19]. Seventh, the immunosensor was reusable and its assay process was simpler than the present method, but its preparation process was complicated [20]. For the molecularly imprinted polymer-based method, its detection spectrum and sensitivity were inferior to the present method [30]. General consideration, the MMIM based semi-homogeneous fluorescence method exhibited better performances than those previous methods.

The inspection of ROX and ASA in animal feed can avoid their residues in foods of animal origin. There have been several methods reported to detect the two analytes in animal feeds, but they required the expensive instrumentals, complicated preparation processes, or long-time of antibody production. The present study synthesized a type of magnetic molecularly imprinted microsphere and a type of carbon dots based fluorescent tracer, and developed a semi-homogeneous fluorescence method on 96-well microplate to detect ROX and ASA in animal feeds. Results showed that the method was simple, rapid, accurate and reusable. Therefore, this method could be used as a practical tool for routine screening of ROX and ASA in animal feed samples.

Yao Jia Peng: Methodology. Cheng Feng: Methodology. Min Lin: Validation. Ge Lin Liu: Methodology. Yan Su: Methodology. Hao Zhe Hou: Methodology. Jian Ping Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111222.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Environ. Health Perspect.
